quiz_questions.question_text: What is alternating current (AC)?

options.text: Direct current
Constant current
Current and voltage that vary sinusoidally
Current that only flows one way

quiz_questions.explanation: AC current and voltage vary sinusoidally: V = V₀ sin(ωt), I = I₀ sin(ωt).

quiz_questions.question_text: What are RMS values?

options.text: The lowest instantaneous values in a cycle
The peak amplitude values of the waveform
Equivalent DC values for calculating power
The time-averaged values over a full cycle

quiz_questions.explanation: RMS values are the equivalent DC values for power calculations: V_rms = V₀/√2, I_rms = I₀/√2.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a resistor, voltage and current are:

options.text: 90° out of phase
In phase
45° out of phase
180° out of phase

quiz_questions.explanation: In a resistor, voltage and current are in phase — they reach their peaks at the same time.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a capacitor, current:

options.text: Is in phase with voltage
Leads voltage by 90°
Leads voltage by 180°
Lags voltage by 90°

quiz_questions.explanation: In a capacitor, current leads voltage by 90°. Remember: 'ICE' — In a Capacitor, current (I) leads EMF (E).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is impedance?

options.text: Inductive reactance only
DC resistance only
Capacitive reactance only
Total opposition to AC current

quiz_questions.explanation: Impedance Z = √(R² + (X_L − X_C)²) is the total opposition to AC current.

quiz_questions.question_text: At resonance in an RLC circuit:

options.text: Impedance is minimum (Z = R), current is maximum
Current is zero
Voltage is zero
Impedance is maximum

quiz_questions.explanation: At resonance, X_L = X_C, so impedance equals just R (minimum) and current is maximum.

quiz_questions.question_text: A step-up transformer:

options.text: Decreases voltage
Increases current
Has no effect
Increases voltage (N₂ > N₁)

quiz_questions.explanation: A step-up transformer has more turns in the secondary than the primary (N₂ > N₁), increasing voltage.

quiz_questions.question_text: In an inductor, current:

options.text: Leads voltage by 90°
Is in phase with voltage
Lags voltage by 90°
Lags voltage by 180°

quiz_questions.explanation: In an inductor, current lags voltage by 90°. Remember: 'ELI' — EMF (E) leads current (I) in an Inductor (L).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the relationship between peak voltage and RMS voltage?

options.text: V_rms = V₀ × √2
V_rms = V₀ / √2
V_rms = V₀ / 2
V_rms = V₀ × 2

quiz_questions.explanation: V_rms = V₀/√2 ≈ 0.707 × V₀. This is the effective DC equivalent voltage for power calculations.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the resonant frequency formula for an RLC circuit?

options.text: f = 2π√(LC)
f = LC/(2π)
f = 2πLC

quiz_questions.explanation: Resonance occurs when ω = 1/√(LC), giving f = 1/(2π√(LC)). At this frequency, X_L = X_C.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'alternating current' (AC)?

options.text: Electric current that periodically reverses direction in a sinusoidal pattern
Electric current that flows in one direction continuously without reversing
Electric current that only flows when a switch is repeatedly turned on and off
Electric current that gradually increases in strength over time without limit

quiz_questions.explanation: Alternating current periodically reverses direction, typically following a sinusoidal pattern. The voltage and current oscillate between positive and negative values.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'RMS' stand for and what does it represent?

options.text: Root Mean Square — the effective DC equivalent value for power calculations
Relative Maximum Signal — the highest voltage reached during a single cycle
Resistance Measurement Standard — the standard way to measure AC resistance
Rapid Mode Switching — how quickly AC changes between positive and negative

quiz_questions.explanation: RMS stands for Root Mean Square. It represents the equivalent DC value that would dissipate the same power in a resistor as the AC signal.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'reactance' in an AC circuit?

options.text: The opposition to AC current from capacitors or inductors but not resistors
The total opposition to current flow including resistance and all components
The power dissipated as heat in a purely resistive AC circuit component
The frequency at which an RLC circuit reaches its maximum current value

quiz_questions.explanation: Reactance is the opposition to AC current from capacitors (X_C = 1/(ωC)) or inductors (X_L = ωL). Unlike resistance, reactance depends on frequency and does not dissipate energy as heat.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'impedance' (Z) in an AC circuit?

options.text: The opposition from resistance only, ignoring capacitors and inductors
The total opposition to AC current combining resistance and reactance
The power factor of the circuit relating real and apparent power
The peak voltage divided by the peak current at any given moment

quiz_questions.explanation: Impedance is the total opposition to AC current: Z = √(R² + (X_L − X_C)²). It combines resistance (energy-dissipating) with reactance (energy-storing).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'resonance' mean in an RLC circuit?

options.text: The frequency at which inductive and capacitive reactances are equal and cancel
The frequency at which the circuit stops working and no current flows at all
The frequency at which all energy is dissipated as heat in the resistor
The frequency at which the voltage and current both drop to exactly zero

quiz_questions.explanation: Resonance occurs when X_L = X_C (ωL = 1/(ωC)). The reactances cancel, impedance is at its minimum (Z = R), and current is at its maximum.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'frequency' in the context of AC?

options.text: The number of complete cycles of AC voltage or current per second in hertz
The maximum voltage that the AC source can produce during each cycle
The time it takes for one complete cycle of the alternating waveform
The ratio of peak voltage to RMS voltage in an alternating current signal

quiz_questions.explanation: Frequency (f) is the number of complete cycles per second, measured in hertz (Hz). Standard household AC in the US is 60 Hz; in many other countries it is 50 Hz.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'period' of an AC signal?

options.text: The maximum amplitude of the voltage waveform measured from zero to peak
The time for one complete cycle of the alternating voltage or current signal
The total duration that AC power is supplied to a circuit before it stops
The phase difference between voltage and current in an RLC circuit loop

quiz_questions.explanation: The period (T) is the time for one complete cycle: T = 1/f. For 60 Hz AC, T = 1/60 ≈ 0.0167 seconds.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'phase difference' in an AC circuit?

options.text: The time delay between voltage and current waveforms expressed in degrees
The difference in amplitude between the voltage and current peak values
The difference in frequency between two AC signals in the same circuit
The physical distance between two components connected in the circuit

quiz_questions.explanation: Phase difference is the angular offset between voltage and current waveforms. In a resistor it is 0°, in a capacitor current leads by 90°, and in an inductor current lags by 90°.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'capacitive reactance'?

options.text: The opposition to AC current by a capacitor: X_C = 1/(ωC), decreasing with frequency
The opposition to AC current by an inductor: X_L = ωL, increasing with frequency
The DC resistance of a capacitor when connected to a direct current source
The total impedance of a circuit containing only capacitors and no resistors

quiz_questions.explanation: Capacitive reactance X_C = 1/(ωC) = 1/(2πfC). It decreases as frequency increases because a capacitor passes high-frequency signals more easily.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'inductive reactance'?

options.text: The opposition to DC current by an inductor in a circuit at zero frequency
The opposition to AC current by an inductor: X_L = ωL, increasing with frequency
The energy stored in a capacitor's electric field during each AC cycle
The total resistance of a coil including its wire resistance and inductance

quiz_questions.explanation: Inductive reactance X_L = ωL = 2πfL. It increases with frequency because the inductor opposes rapid changes in current more strongly.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures the peak voltage of an AC outlet as 170 V. What is the RMS voltage?

options.text: The RMS voltage is approximately 85 V
The RMS voltage is approximately 120 V
The RMS voltage is approximately 170 V
The RMS voltage is approximately 240 V

quiz_questions.explanation: V_rms = V₀/√2 = 170/1.414 ≈ 120 V. This is why US outlets are rated at 120 V — that is the RMS value.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student connects only a capacitor to an AC source and increases the frequency. What happens to the current?

options.text: Current decreases because the capacitor blocks higher frequencies
Current increases because capacitive reactance decreases with frequency
Current stays constant regardless of the frequency of the AC source
Current drops to zero because capacitors cannot pass alternating current

quiz_questions.explanation: X_C = 1/(2πfC). As frequency increases, X_C decreases, so more current flows. Capacitors pass high frequencies easily and block low frequencies.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student connects only an inductor to an AC source and increases the frequency. What happens to the current?

options.text: Current increases because inductors pass higher frequencies more easily
Current stays exactly the same at all frequencies for an inductor
Current decreases because inductive reactance increases with frequency
Current increases to infinity as the frequency approaches resonance

quiz_questions.explanation: X_L = 2πfL. As frequency increases, X_L increases, reducing current. Inductors block high frequencies and pass low frequencies — opposite behavior to capacitors.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student tunes a radio by adjusting a variable capacitor in an RLC circuit. What are they doing?

options.text: Changing the resistance to filter out unwanted radio stations
Changing the resonant frequency to match the desired station's broadcast frequency
Increasing the power output of the radio to receive a stronger signal
Adjusting the volume of the radio by changing the current through speakers

quiz_questions.explanation: Changing the capacitance changes the resonant frequency f = 1/(2π√(LC)). When the circuit's resonant frequency matches a station's broadcast frequency, maximum current flows, and that station is selected.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices their phone charger gets warm during charging. In AC circuit terms, what causes this?

options.text: The resonance in the charger circuit creates excessive current and heat
Power dissipated by resistive components in the charger is converted to heat
The capacitors inside the charger overheat from storing too much charge
The AC from the wall outlet is inherently hot and transfers heat to charger

quiz_questions.explanation: The charger contains resistive components (including transformer windings and voltage regulators). Power dissipated in these components (P = I²R) is converted to heat, warming the charger.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student learns that power lines transmit electricity at very high voltages. Why is this more efficient?

options.text: Higher voltage means higher frequency which travels faster through the wires
Higher voltage allows lower current which reduces I²R power losses in the wires
Higher voltage makes the wires stronger and less likely to break from weather
Higher voltage is required because electricity cannot travel long distances at low voltage

quiz_questions.explanation: P = IV, so for the same power, higher voltage means lower current. Power loss in wires is P_loss = I²R, so lower current dramatically reduces energy wasted as heat in the transmission lines.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student builds an RLC circuit and measures that at 500 Hz the current is at its maximum. What is the resonant frequency of this circuit?

options.text: The resonant frequency is 250 Hz
The resonant frequency is 500 Hz
The resonant frequency is 1000 Hz
The resonant frequency cannot be determined from this information

quiz_questions.explanation: At resonance, the current is at maximum because impedance is at minimum (Z = R). If current peaks at 500 Hz, that is the resonant frequency.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student wonders why household electricity uses AC instead of DC. What is a key advantage of AC?

options.text: AC is safer to use than DC at the same voltage level
AC voltage can be easily stepped up or down with transformers for efficient transmission
AC produces more power than DC for the same voltage and current values
AC is the only type of current that can flow through wires over long distances

quiz_questions.explanation: AC can be easily transformed to different voltage levels using transformers. This allows high-voltage transmission (low losses) and step-down to safe household voltages. DC cannot be easily transformed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures the household AC frequency as 60 Hz. How many times does the current reverse direction each second?

options.text: It reverses direction 60 times per second total
It reverses direction 120 times per second total
It reverses direction 30 times per second total
It reverses direction 240 times per second total

quiz_questions.explanation: At 60 Hz, there are 60 complete cycles per second. In each cycle, the current goes positive then negative, reversing direction twice per cycle: 60 × 2 = 120 reversals per second.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student designs a filter circuit to block high-frequency noise from a signal. Should they use a capacitor or inductor in series with the signal?

options.text: A capacitor in series, because capacitors block low frequencies and pass high frequencies
An inductor in series, because inductors block high frequencies and pass low frequencies
A resistor in series, because resistors block all frequencies equally and remove noise
Neither will work; only digital filters can remove high-frequency noise from signals

quiz_questions.explanation: An inductor's reactance X_L = 2πfL increases with frequency. Placed in series, it blocks high-frequency noise while allowing the desired low-frequency signal to pass through. This is a low-pass filter.
